documentRead.whatItIs: A research-grounded report from the Learning Policy Institute and EdPrepLab laying out five design principles for teacher preparation programs built on the science of learning and development (SoLD).
documentRead.audience: Teacher educators, preparation program leaders, accrediting/licensing bodies, and education policymakers.
documentRead.coreMessage: Teacher preparation should be redesigned around how people actually learn and develop — grounding candidates in deep knowledge of learners, equitable mindsets, experiential learning, pedagogical modeling, and communities of practice.

stats.fact: About 46% of U.S. children each year are exposed to violence, crime, abuse, or psychological trauma, homelessness, or food insecurity.
In a survey, 100% of Alverno teacher candidates said they often experienced collaborative learning in their coursework and felt prepared to set norms for a productive classroom community.
When teachers learned about similarities with students, Black and Latino/a students' grades rose enough to close the grade gap with White students by over 60%.
A meta-analysis of 99 studies found the affective quality of teacher–student relationships was significantly related to student engagement and achievement.
An estimated 3.3 million students are suspended and miss school time each year, disproportionately students of color and students with disabilities.
An action-research survey found about 10% of the June Jordan School for Equity student population was undocumented — a figure that surprised staff.

stories.subject: Luisa Costa's Spanish/Farsi model lesson at Bank Street
stories.whatHappened: An instructor teaches a fruit-vocabulary lesson entirely in Spanish (and previously Farsi) to put English-speaking candidates in the shoes of a language learner, modeling scaffolding strategies.

stories.subject: Ted Pollen's 4th-grade math class (Bank Street/Midtown West)
stories.whatHappened: A cooperating teacher guides students through a hands-on study of range, mean, median, and mode using their own measured heights, scaffolding with questions while student teachers observe.

stories.subject: San Francisco Teacher Residency seminar with JJSE students
stories.whatHappened: High school students of color teach residents a lesson on supporting undocumented students, flipping roles; one student, Wendy, shares her personal story about her undocumented parents.

stories.subject: Lily, teacher candidate at Alverno/Walker Elementary
stories.whatHappened: A student teacher runs a collaborative iPad-storage math lesson for 4th-graders using the CUBE strategy and shared Google Docs to build problem-solving and collaboration skills.

stories.subject: Hinkley High School PDS partnership with CU Denver
stories.whatHappened: Candidate Maria Sanchez co-teaches a probability unit with clinical teacher Joan Simmons, using gallery walks and group reasoning in a school that centers relationships and deeper learning.

stories.subject: Montclair State–Newark (East Side High) residency
stories.whatHappened: A decade-long residency partnership raised teacher quality, hired program graduates, and improved student achievement and STEM enrollment.

stories.subject: Vanderbilt video analysis (John, Peter)
stories.whatHappened: Candidates analyze videos of their own tutoring/teaching to shift intellectual work to students and leverage multilingual resources.

stories.subject: Art Backpack program at Benjamin Franklin Elementary (Newark/Montclair)
stories.whatHappened: A family-school-university intervention sends art activities home; participating students consistently scored higher on standardized tests.

stories.subject: Sandra at Stanford (STEP) place-value lesson
stories.whatHappened: A candidate practices asset-based talk moves in a 3rd-grade place-value lesson after analyzing classroom video in her methods course.

reasons.length: At ~30,742 words with five detailed principles and many vignettes, this is a dense report that warrants a long, well-structured page.
reasons.summaryChoice: The document has a strong ~1,715-word executive summary that already frames all five principles cleanly — use it rather than a synthesized AI blurb.
reasons.coverLayout: There's no compelling cover image described, and the value is in the ideas and framework, so lead with text.

aiSummary.note: Only needed if you want a shorter plain-language hook; the exec summary already does the job.

execSummary.note: Ready-made and authoritative — it names the five principles and the whole-child framing.

highlights.note: Pull the five design principles plus a few punchy stats (46% trauma exposure, 60%+ grade-gap closure) as scannable takeaways.

findings.note: The 'what the science says' sections give solid research-backed points worth listing.

toc.note: Long, multi-section document — a table of contents helps readers navigate the five principles.

storytelling.note: This is the report's real strength — rich classroom vignettes from Bank Street, Alverno, SFTR, Hinkley and more make it vivid.

download.note: A 68-page report people will want to keep — offer the full PDF.

secondary.note: Link the companion Design Principles for Schools and EdPrepLab resources for interested readers.

share.note: Fine to include, but not central for a policy/practitioner audience.
